            end
        end
    end
    
    return positions
end

5.10 7.5 Adaptive Model Selection

Real systems require dynamic decisions about when to use ABM versus continuum descriptions. This necessitates error indicators and adaptive algorithms that can switch between models based on local conditions.

5.10.1 7.5.1 Error Indicators

Define local error indicators that measure when continuum approximations break down:

  1. Fluctuation indicator: \[\eta_{\text{fluct}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\sqrt{\text{Var}[N_{\text{local}}(\mathbf{x})]}}{\langle N_{\text{local}}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle}\]

  2. Gradient indicator: \[\eta_{\text{grad}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{|\nabla \rho(\mathbf{x})|}{\rho(\mathbf{x})} \ell_{\text{interaction}}\]

  3. Time scale indicator: \[\eta_{\text{time}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\tau_{\text{reaction}}(\mathbf{x})}{\tau_{\text{diffusion}}(\mathbf{x})}\]

where \(\ell_{\text{interaction}}\) is the typical interaction range between agents.

Definition 7.5 (Adaptive Refinement Criterion). Switch from continuum to ABM description at location \(\mathbf{x}\) if: \[\max(\eta_{\text{fluct}}, \eta_{\text{grad}}, \eta_{\text{time}}) > \theta_{\text{critical}}\] where \(\theta_{\text{critical}}\) is a user-defined threshold.

5.10.2 7.5.2 Dynamic Domain Decomposition

The domains \(\Omega_{\text{ABM}}\) and \(\Omega_{\text{PDE}}\) evolve dynamically based on error indicators:

mutable struct AdaptiveDomain
    abm_regions::Vector{BoundingBox}
    pde_regions::Vector{BoundingBox}  
    interface_cells::Vector{Int}
    error_threshold::Float64
    refinement_buffer::Float64  # safety margin around high-error regions
end

function update_domain_decomposition!(domain, error_field, grid)
    # Identify cells requiring ABM resolution
    high_error_cells = Int[]
    
    for (i, cell) in enumerate(grid.cells)
        if error_field[i] > domain.error_threshold
            push!(high_error_cells, i)
        end
    end
    
    # Add buffer region around high-error cells
    buffered_cells = add_buffer_region(high_error_cells, grid, domain.refinement_buffer)
    
    # Update ABM and PDE regions
    domain.abm_regions = cells_to_bounding_boxes(buffered_cells, grid)
    domain.pde_regions = complement_regions(domain.abm_regions, grid.domain)
    
    # Identify interface cells
    domain.interface_cells = find_interface_cells(domain.abm_regions, domain.pde_regions, grid)
    
    return domain
end

function transfer_agents_across_interface!(abm_agents, pde_solution, domain, grid)
    # ABM → PDE: Remove agents entering PDE regions
    agents_to_remove = Int[]
    mass_transfer_to_pde = Dict{Int, Float64}()
    
    for (i, agent) in enumerate(abm_agents)
        if !point_in_abm_region(agent.pos, domain.abm_regions)
            cell_idx = find_cell(agent.pos, grid)
            mass_transfer_to_pde[cell_idx] = get(mass_transfer_to_pde, cell_idx, 0.0) + 1.0
            push!(agents_to_remove, i)
        end
    end
    
    # Remove agents and update PDE solution
    deleteat!(abm_agents, agents_to_remove)
    for (cell_idx, mass) in mass_transfer_to_pde
        cell_volume = compute_cell_volume(grid, cell_idx)
        pde_solution[cell_idx] += mass / cell_volume
    end
    
    # PDE → ABM: Create agents for mass entering ABM regions
    for cell_idx in domain.interface_cells
        if cell_in_pde_region(cell_idx, domain.pde_regions)
            # Compute flux into ABM region
            flux = compute_interface_flux(pde_solution, cell_idx, grid, domain)
            
            if flux > 0
                n_new_agents = round(Int, flux * grid.dt)
                for _ in 1:n_new_agents
                    pos = sample_position_near_interface(cell_idx, grid, domain)
                    push!(abm_agents, create_agent(pos))
                end
                
                # Remove mass from PDE solution
                cell_volume = compute_cell_volume(grid, cell_idx)
                pde_solution[cell_idx] -= n_new_agents / cell_volume
            end
        end
    end
end

5.11 7.6 Applications: Tumor Growth

Tumor growth exemplifies the need for multiscale modeling: individual cell behavior drives invasion at the tumor margin, while bulk dynamics follow population-level equations. This section develops a comprehensive hybrid model coupling ABM at the invasion front with reaction-diffusion equations in the tumor core.

5.11.1 7.6.1 Mathematical Framework

The tumor consists of proliferating cells with density \(\rho(\mathbf{x}, t)\) and nutrients with concentration \(c(\mathbf{x}, t)\). In regions of high cell density (tumor core), continuum equations suffice:

\[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (D_\rho(\rho, c) \nabla \rho) + G(\rho, c) - \delta(\rho, c)\]

\[\frac{\partial c}{\partial t} = D_c \nabla^2 c - \Lambda(\rho) c + S_c(\mathbf{x})\]

where: - \(D_\rho(\rho, c)\): density-dependent cell diffusivity - \(G(\rho, c)\): proliferation rate - \(\delta(\rho, c)\): death rate
- \(\Lambda(\rho)\): nutrient consumption rate - \(S_c(\mathbf{x})\): nutrient supply (e.g., from vasculature)

At the invasion front, where cell density is low and stochastic effects dominate, individual agents follow:

\[d\mathbf{x}_i = [\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\text{chem}}(\nabla c) + \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\text{prolif}}(\rho_{\text{local}}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\text{random}}] dt + \sqrt{2D_{\text{cell}}} d\mathbf{W}_i\]

with birth/death events at stochastic rates depending on local conditions.

Definition 7.6 (Tumor Front Criterion). The tumor-host interface is defined by the level set \(\rho(\mathbf{x}, t) = \rho_{\text{critical}}\) where \(\rho_{\text{critical}} \ll \rho_{\text{max}}\) is chosen such that stochastic effects become

6 Chapter 8: Validation of Coarse-Grained Models

The derivation of mean-field theories and continuum descriptions from agent-based models represents a powerful theoretical framework, but these coarse-grained models are only as valuable as their accuracy in representing the underlying microscopic dynamics. This chapter develops systematic approaches for validating coarse-grained models against their agent-based counterparts, identifying when and why these approximations fail, and quantifying the errors inherent in the coarse-graining process.

The validation of coarse-grained models presents unique challenges that distinguish it from traditional model validation. We are not comparing models to experimental data, but rather examining the fidelity with which a simplified mathematical description captures the statistical properties of a more complex computational model. This comparison spans multiple temporal and spatial scales, requiring sophisticated statistical and information-theoretic tools.

6.1 Ensemble Statistics and Comparative Analysis

6.1.1 Statistical Moments and Distribution Comparison

The most fundamental approach to validation involves comparing statistical moments between the agent-based model and its mean-field approximation. Consider an agent-based model with state variables \(\mathbf{X}(t) = (X_1(t), X_2(t), \ldots, X_n(t))\) representing population counts or densities of different agent types, and let \(\mathbf{x}(t)\) denote the corresponding mean-field solution.

Definition 8.1 (Moment Error). For an agent-based model with state \(\mathbf{X}(t)\) and mean-field approximation \(\mathbf{x}(t)\), the \(k\)-th moment error at time \(t\) is defined as \[E_k(t) = \left| \mathbb{E}[X_i^k(t)] - x_i^k(t) \right|\] for each component \(i\), where the expectation is taken over ensemble realizations of the agent-based model.

The zeroth and first moments provide the most direct comparison, as mean-field theory by construction matches the first moment \(\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}(t)]\) in the limit of infinite population size. Higher-order moments reveal the limitations of mean-field approximations, particularly their inability to capture fluctuations and correlations.

For a birth-death process with rates \(\lambda(n)\) and \(\mu(n)\), the mean-field equation is \(\dot{x} = \lambda(x) - \mu(x)\). The second moment evolution follows:

\[\frac{d}{dt}\mathbb{E}[N^2(t)] = 2\mathbb{E}[N(t)(\lambda(N(t)) - \mu(N(t)))] + \mathbb{E}[\lambda(N(t)) + \mu(N(t))]\]

The variance \(\text{Var}[N(t)] = \mathbb{E}[N^2(t)] - \mathbb{E}[N(t)]^2\) captures fluctuations absent in the mean-field description.

using DifferentialEquations, Distributions, Statistics, StatsPlots

# Birth-death process ABM simulation
function simulate_birthdeath_abm(n0, λ, μ, tmax, nruns=1000)
    times = 0:0.1:tmax
    trajectories = zeros(nruns, length(times))
    
    for run in 1:nruns
        n = n0
        t = 0.0
        tidx = 1
        
        while t < tmax && tidx <= length(times)
            # Record state at observation times
            while tidx <= length(times) && times[tidx] <= t
                trajectories[run, tidx] = n
                tidx += 1
            end
            
            if n == 0
                break  # Extinction
            end
            
            # Gillespie step
            birth_rate = λ * n
            death_rate = μ * n
            total_rate = birth_rate + death_rate
            
            dt = -log(rand()) / total_rate
            t += dt
            
            if rand() < birth_rate / total_rate
                n += 1  # Birth
            else
                n -= 1  # Death
            end
        end
        
        # Fill remaining times if extinction occurred
        for i in tidx:length(times)
            trajectories[run, i] = n
        end
    end
    
    return times, trajectories
end

# Mean-field solution
function solve_birthdeath_mf(n0, λ, μ, tmax)
    if λ == μ
        return t -> n0  # Neutral case
    else
        return t -> n0 * exp((λ - μ) * t)
    end
end

# Parameters
n0, λ, μ, tmax = 50, 0.1, 0.12, 20.0

# Simulate ABM
times, trajectories = simulate_birthdeath_abm(n0, λ, μ, tmax)

# Compute ensemble statistics
mean_traj = mean(trajectories, dims=1)[:]
var_traj = var(trajectories, dims=1)[:]
std_traj = sqrt.(var_traj)

# Mean-field solution
mf_solution = solve_birthdeath_mf(n0, λ, μ, tmax)
mf_values = [mf_solution(t) for t in times]

# Plot comparison
plot(times, mean_traj, ribbon=std_traj, alpha=0.3, 
     label="ABM mean ± std", linewidth=2)
plot!(times, mf_values, label="Mean-field", 
      linewidth=2, linestyle=:dash)
xlabel!("Time")
ylabel!("Population size")
title!("ABM vs Mean-Field: Birth-Death Process")

6.1.2 Distribution Shape Analysis

Beyond moments, the full probability distribution provides deeper insights into model validity. The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test and other distribution comparison methods can quantify differences between ABM and mean-field predictions.

Definition 8.2 (Distribution Distance). The Wasserstein-2 distance between the ABM distribution \(P_{ABM}(t)\) and mean-field approximation \(P_{MF}(t)\) at time \(t\) is \[W_2(P_{ABM}, P_{MF}) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Gamma(P_{ABM}, P_{MF})} \left( \int |x - y|^2 d\gamma(x,y) \right)^{1/2}\] where \(\Gamma(P_{ABM}, P_{MF})\) is the set of all couplings between the two distributions.

6.2 Transient Dynamics and Relaxation Behavior

6.2.1 Approach to Equilibrium

The transient behavior often reveals the most significant differences between agent-based models and their mean-field approximations. While both may converge to the same steady state in the thermodynamic limit, their relaxation dynamics can differ substantially due to finite-size effects and stochastic fluctuations.

Consider a system approaching equilibrium with characteristic relaxation time \(\tau_{ABM}\) for the agent-based model and \(\tau_{MF}\) for the mean-field approximation. The ratio \(\tau_{ABM}/\tau_{MF}\) quantifies how stochastic effects modify relaxation rates.

For a linear system near equilibrium, fluctuations modify the effective dynamics through noise-induced transitions. The Kramers escape time for transitions over energy barriers scales exponentially with barrier height, leading to dramatically different relaxation behaviors in finite populations.

using LinearAlgebra

# Multi-population Lotka-Volterra system
function simulate_lotka_volterra_abm(x0, y0, α, β, γ, δ, tmax, nruns=100)
    dt = 0.01
    times = 0:dt:tmax
    
    x_trajs = zeros(nruns, length(times))
    y_trajs = zeros(nruns, length(times))
    
    for run in 1:nruns
        x, y = x0, y0
        
        for (i, t) in enumerate(times)
            x_trajs[run, i] = x
            y_trajs[run, i] = y
            
            if i < length(times)
                # Stochastic updates using Euler-Maruyama
                dx_det = α * x - β * x * y
                dy_det = δ * x * y - γ * y
                
                # Add noise proportional to sqrt(rates)
                noise_scale = sqrt(dt)
                dx_noise = sqrt(max(0, α * x + β * x * y)) * randn() * noise_scale
                dy_noise = sqrt(max(0, δ * x * y + γ * y)) * randn() * noise_scale
                
                x += dx_det * dt + dx_noise
                y += dy_det * dt + dy_noise
                
                # Prevent negative populations
                x = max(0, x)
                y = max(0, y)
            end
        end
    end
    
    return times, x_trajs, y_trajs
end

# Mean-field Lotka-Volterra
function lotka_volterra_mf!(du, u, p, t)
    α, β, γ, δ = p
    x, y = u
    du[1] = α * x - β * x * y
    du[2] = δ * x * y - γ * y
end

# Parameters and simulation
α, β, γ, δ = 1.0, 0.5, 1.0, 0.5
x0, y0 = 10.0, 10.0
tmax = 10.0

# ABM simulation
times, x_abm, y_abm = simulate_lotka_volterra_abm(x0, y0, α, β, γ, δ, tmax)

# Mean-field solution
u0 = [x0, y0]
p = [α, β, γ, δ]
prob = ODEProblem(lotka_volterra_mf!, u0, (0.0, tmax), p)
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=times)

# Compute relaxation times by analyzing approach to quasi-periodic orbit
function compute_relaxation_time(trajectory, times)
    # Simple approach: time to reach 90% of final oscillation amplitude
    final_amp = std(trajectory[end-100:end])
    target_amp = 0.9 * final_amp
    
    for i in 200:length(trajectory)-100
        if std(trajectory[i-50:i+50]) >= target_amp
            return times[i]
        end
    end
    return times[end]
end

# Analyze individual ABM trajectories
relaxation_times_abm = [compute_relaxation_time(x_abm[i, :], times) 
                        for i in 1:size(x_abm, 1)]
mean_relaxation_abm = mean(relaxation_times_abm)

# Mean-field relaxation (approximate)
mf_relaxation = compute_relaxation_time(sol[1, :], times)

println("Mean ABM relaxation time: $(mean_relaxation_abm)")
println("Mean-field relaxation time: $(mf_relaxation)")
println("Ratio τ_ABM/τ_MF: $(mean_relaxation_abm/mf_relaxation)")

6.3 Steady States and Critical Phenomena

6.3.1 Phase Transitions and Critical Points

Coarse-grained models often fail most dramatically near critical points and phase transitions. The mean-field approximation typically predicts sharp transitions that are smoothed by fluctuations in finite-size systems. Critical exponents derived from mean-field theory frequently differ from those observed in agent-based models and experiments.

Theorem 8.1 (Critical Fluctuations). Near a critical point of a phase transition, the correlation length \(\xi\) diverges as \(\xi \sim |T - T_c|^{-\nu}\), where \(\nu\) is the critical exponent. Mean-field theory predicts \(\nu_{MF} = 1/2\), while the actual exponent depends on the system’s dimensionality and universality class.

Proof. The mean-field approximation neglects spatial correlations, treating each site as experiencing the average field from all others. This leads to the Landau-Ginzburg free energy \(F[\phi] = \int d^d x [\frac{1}{2}(\nabla \phi)^2 + \frac{r}{2}\phi^2 + \frac{u}{4}\phi^4]\) with \(r \propto (T - T_c)\). The correlation function \(G(r) = \langle \phi(0)\phi(r) \rangle\) satisfies \((-\nabla^2 + r)G(r) = \delta(r)\), giving \(G(r) \sim e^{-r/\xi}/r^{d-2}\) with \(\xi = r^{-1/2}\), hence \(\nu_{MF} = 1/2\). However, fluctuations modify this behavior, leading to dimension-dependent corrections through the renormalization group flow. □

6.3.2 Extinction and Rare Events

Extinction events provide a stringent test of model validity. Mean-field models typically predict exponential decay to extinction, while stochastic agent-based models exhibit more complex behavior including extinction time distributions with heavy tails.

The mean first passage time for extinction in a birth-death process follows:

\[\mathbb{E}[\tau_{ext}|N(0) = n] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\mu k} \prod_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\lambda j}{\mu j}\]

This formula reveals that extinction times can be much longer in the stochastic model than predicted by mean-field theory, particularly when birth and death rates are nearly balanced.

using Distributions

# Analyze extinction times in birth-death process
function extinction_time_distribution(n0, λ, μ, nruns=1000)
    extinction_times = Float64[]
    
    for run in 1:nruns
        n = n0
        t = 0.0
        
        while n > 0
            birth_rate = λ * n
            death_rate = μ * n  
            total_rate = birth_rate + death_rate
            
            dt = -log(rand()) / total_rate
            t += dt
            
            if rand() < birth_rate / total_rate
                n += 1
            else
                n -= 1
            end
            
            # Safety check for very long simulations
            if t > 1000
                push!(extinction_times, Inf)
                break
            end
        end
        
        if n == 0
            push!(extinction_times, t)
        end
    end
    
    return extinction_times[isfinite.(extinction_times)]
end

# Theoretical mean extinction time for birth-death process
function theoretical_extinction_time(n0, λ, μ)
    if λ >= μ
        return Inf  # Population grows on average
    end
    
    ρ = λ / μ
    if ρ == 1
        return n0 / μ  # Neutral case
    else
        return (1 - ρ^n0) / (μ * (1 - ρ) * ρ^n0) - n0 / (μ * (1 - ρ))
    end
end

# Compare extinction times
n0, λ, μ = 20, 0.08, 0.10
extinction_times = extinction_time_distribution(n0, λ, μ)
theoretical_mean = theoretical_extinction_time(n0, λ, μ)

println("Simulated mean extinction time: $(mean(extinction_times))")
println("Theoretical mean extinction time: $(theoretical_mean)")
println("Relative error: $(abs(mean(extinction_times) - theoretical_mean) / theoretical_mean)")

# Plot distribution
histogram(extinction_times, bins=50, alpha=0.7, normalize=:pdf,
          label="ABM simulation")
vline!([theoretical_mean], label="Theoretical mean", linewidth=2)
xlabel!("Extinction time")
ylabel!("Probability density")
title!("Extinction Time Distribution")

6.4 Finite-Size Effects

6.4.1 System Size Scaling

Finite-size effects represent systematic deviations from mean-field predictions that scale with inverse powers of the system size \(N\). These effects become particularly pronounced near critical points and in systems with multiple stable states.

Definition 8.3 (Finite-Size Scaling). A quantity \(Q\) exhibits finite-size scaling if \(Q(N) = Q_\infty + A N^{-\alpha} + B N^{-\beta} + \ldots\) where \(Q_\infty\) is the thermodynamic limit value and \(\alpha, \beta > 0\) are scaling exponents.

The leading finite-size correction often comes from fluctuations, giving \(\alpha = 1/2\) for many quantities. However, near critical points, anomalous scaling can occur with non-trivial exponents related to the correlation length and system dimensionality.

6.4.2 Boundary Effects and Geometry

The geometry and boundary conditions of the agent-based model significantly influence the validity of continuum approximations. Mean-field theories assume infinite, well-mixed systems, while real agent-based models have finite domains with specific boundary conditions.

Consider agents moving on a lattice with reflecting boundaries. The effective diffusion coefficient near boundaries differs from the bulk value, leading to inhomogeneous concentration profiles not captured by simple continuum models.

using SparseArrays

# Spatial agent-based model with diffusion
function spatial_diffusion_abm(L, N, D, dt, tmax)
    # Initialize agents randomly on LxL lattice
    positions = [rand(1:L, 2) for _ in 1:N]
    times = 0:dt:tmax
    density_profiles = zeros(length(times), L)
    
    for (tidx, t) in enumerate(times)
        # Record density profile
        for pos in positions
            density_profiles[tidx, pos[1]] += 1
        end
        
        # Move agents (random walk with reflecting boundaries)
        if tidx < length(times)
            for i in 1:N
                if rand() < D * dt  # Probability of movement
                    direction = rand(1:4)  # Four directions
                    new_pos = copy(positions[i])
                    
                    if direction == 1 && new_pos[1] < L
                        new_pos[1] += 1
                    elseif direction == 2 && new_pos[1] > 1
                        new_pos[1] -= 1  
                    elseif direction == 3 && new_pos[2] < L
                        new_pos[2] += 1
                    elseif direction == 4 && new_pos[2] > 1
                        new_pos[2] -= 1
                    end
                    
                    positions[i] = new_pos
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    return times, density_profiles ./ N  # Normalize to get density
end

# Compare with continuum diffusion equation
function solve_diffusion_pde(L, ρ0, D, dt, tmax)
    dx = 1.0  # Lattice spacing
    times = 0:dt:tmax
    x = 1:L
    
    # Initial condition (uniform density)
    u = fill(ρ0, L)
    
    # Finite difference matrix for diffusion with reflecting boundaries
    A = spdiagm(-1 => ones(L-1), 0 => -2*ones(L), 1 => ones(L-1))
    A[1, 1] = -1  # Reflecting boundary at x=1
    A[L, L] = -1  # Reflecting boundary at x=L
    A *= D / dx^2
    
    density_profiles = zeros(length(times), L)
    
    for (tidx, t) in enumerate(times)
        density_profiles[tidx, :] = u
        
        if tidx < length(times)
            u += A * u * dt  # Forward Euler step
        end
    end
    
    return times, density_profiles
end

# Parameters
L, N, D, dt, tmax = 50, 1000, 1.0, 0.1, 5.0
ρ0 = N / L

# Simulate ABM
times_abm, density_abm = spatial_diffusion_abm(L, N, D, dt, tmax)

# Solve PDE
times_pde, density_pde = solve_diffusion_pde(L, ρ0, D, dt, tmax)

# Compare final density profiles
final_idx = length(times_abm)
plot(1:L, density_abm[final_idx, :], label="ABM", marker=:circle)
plot!(1:L, density_pde[final_idx, :], label="PDE", linewidth=2)
xlabel!("Position")
ylabel!("Density") 
title!("Spatial Density Profile Comparison")

# Compute L2 error as function of time
l2_errors = [sqrt(sum((density_abm[i, :] - density_pde[i, :]).^2)) 
             for i in 1:length(times_abm)]

plot(times_abm, l2_errors)
xlabel!("Time")
ylabel!("L2 error")
title!("Error Evolution: ABM vs PDE")

6.5 Information-Theoretic Measures

6.5.1 Kullback-Leibler Divergence

The Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence provides a principled measure of the difference between probability distributions generated by agent-based models and their mean-field approximations. This information-theoretic approach quantifies how much information is lost in the coarse-graining process.

Definition 8.4 (KL Divergence for Model Comparison). Given probability distributions \(P_{ABM}(x)\) and \(P_{MF}(x)\) for the agent-based model and mean-field approximation respectively, the KL divergence is \[D_{KL}(P_{ABM} \| P_{MF}) = \int P_{ABM}(x) \log \frac{P_{ABM}(x)}{P_{MF}(x)} dx\]

The KL divergence is non-negative and equals zero only when the distributions are identical. Large KL divergence indicates significant information loss in the mean-field approximation.

For discrete state spaces, the KL divergence becomes: \[D_{KL}(P_{ABM} \| P_{MF}) = \sum_{i} P_{ABM}(x_i) \log \frac{P_{ABM}(x_i)}{P_{MF}(x_i)}\]

6.5.2 Mutual Information and Correlation

Mutual information quantifies the amount of statistical dependence between different components of the system, revealing correlations that mean-field approximations typically neglect.

Definition 8.5 (Mutual Information Loss). The mutual information loss in a mean-field approximation is \[\Delta I = I_{ABM}(X_1; X_2) - I_{MF}(X_1; X_2)\] where \(I(X_1; X_2) = D_{KL}(P(X_1, X_2) \| P(X_1)P(X_2))\) measures the deviation from independence.

using Entropy

# Compute KL divergence between ABM and mean-field distributions
function compute_kl_divergence(abm_samples, mf_samples, nbins=50)
    # Create histograms
    min_val = min(minimum(abm_samples), minimum(mf_samples))
    max_val = max(maximum(abm_samples), maximum(mf_samples))
    bin_edges = range(min_val, max_val, length=nbins+1)
    
    # Compute normalized histograms
    abm_hist = fit(Histogram, abm_samples, bin_edges)
    mf_hist = fit(Histogram, mf_samples, bin_edges)
    
    abm_probs = abm_hist.weights ./ sum(abm_hist.weights)
    mf_probs = mf_hist.weights ./ sum(mf_hist.weights)
    
    # Add small epsilon to avoid log(0)
    ε = 1e-10
    abm_probs .+= ε
    mf_probs .+= ε
    
    # Renormalize
    abm_probs ./= sum(abm_probs)
    mf_probs ./= sum(mf_probs)
    
    # Compute KL divergence
    kl_div = sum(abm_probs .* log.(abm_probs ./ mf_probs))
    
    return kl_div, bin_edges[1:end-1], abm_probs, mf_probs
end

# Example: Competition model with different noise levels  
function competition_model_comparison()
    # Parameters
    α1, α2 = 1.0, 0.8  # Growth rates
    β11, β12, β21, β22 = 0.1, 0.15, 0.12, 0.08  # Competition coefficients
    σ = 0.1  # Noise strength
    
    dt = 0.01
    tmax = 20.0
    times = 0:dt:tmax
    
    # ABM with noise
    function simulate_competition_abm(x0, y0, nruns=1000)
        final_states = zeros(nruns, 2)
        
        for run in 1:nruns
            x, y = x0, y0
            
            for t in times[1:end-1]
                dx = (α1 * x - β11 * x^2 - β12 * x * y) * dt + σ * x * sqrt(dt) * randn()
                dy = (α2 * y - β21 * x * y - β22 * y^2) * dt + σ * y * sqrt(dt) * randn()
                
                x = max(0, x + dx)
                y = max(0, y + dy)
            end
            
            final_states[run, :] = [x, y]
        end
        
        return final_states
    end
    
    # Mean-field deterministic solution
    function competition_ode!(du, u, p, t)
        α1, α2, β11, β12, β21, β22 = p
        x, y = u
        du[1] = α1 * x - β11 * x^2 - β12 * x * y
        du[2] = α2 * y - β21 * x * y - β22 * y^2  
    end
    
    # Initial conditions and simulation
    x0, y0 = 5.0, 3.0
    abm_final = simulate_competition_abm(x0, y0)
    
    # Solve mean-field
    u0 = [x0, y0]
    p = [α1, α2, β11, β12, β21, β22]
    prob = ODEProblem(competition_ode!, u0, (0.0, tmax), p)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())
    mf_final = [sol(tmax)[1], sol(tmax)[2]]
    
    # Since mean-field gives point estimate, create artificial samples
    # around the mean-field solution for KL divergence computation
    mf_samples_x = mf_final[1] .+ 0.01 * randn(1000)  # Small artificial noise
    mf_samples_y = mf_final[2] .+ 0.01 * randn(1000)
    
    # Compute KL divergence for each species
    kl_x, _, _, _ = compute_kl_divergence(abm_final[:, 1], mf_samples_x)
    kl_y, _, _, _ = compute_kl_divergence(abm_final[:, 2], mf_samples_y)
    
    println("KL divergence for species 1: $(kl_x)")
    println("KL divergence for species 2: $(kl_y)")
    
    # Plot final state distributions
    scatter(abm_final[:, 1], abm_final[:, 2], alpha=0.6, label="ABM samples")
    scatter!([mf_final[1]], [mf_final[2]], marker=:star, 
             markersize=10, color=:red, label="Mean-field")
    xlabel!("Species 1 density")
    ylabel!("Species 2 density") 
    title!("Final State Distribution Comparison")
    
    return kl_x, kl_y
end

kl_div_x, kl_div_y = competition_model_comparison()

6.6 Calibration and Parameter Estimation

6.6.1 Moment Matching

One approach to improving mean-field approximations involves calibrating parameters to match higher-order moments from agent-based simulations. This systematic parameter adjustment can partially compensate for the neglect of correlations and fluctuations.

Definition 8.6 (Moment-Matched Calibration). Given target moments \(\mu_k^{(ABM)}\) from agent-based simulations, the moment-matched parameters \(\theta^*\) solve: \[\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta \sum_{k=1}^K w_k |\mu_k^{(MF)}(\theta) - \mu_k^{(ABM)}|^2\] where \(w_k\) are weights reflecting the relative importance of different moments.

6.6.2 Maximum Likelihood Estimation

A more sophisticated approach uses maximum likelihood estimation to fit mean-field parameters to ABM trajectory data. This requires specifying a likelihood function that accounts for the stochastic nature of the agent-based model.

For a mean-field model with additive noise \(d\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, \theta)dt + \boldsymbol{\sigma}dW_t\), the log-likelihood of observing trajectory \(\{\mathbf{x}(t_i)\}\) is:

\[\ell(\theta) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \left[ (\mathbf{x}(t_{i+1}) - \mathbf{x}(t_i) - \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t_i), \theta)\Delta t)^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}(t_{i+1}) - \mathbf{x}(t_i) - \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}(t_i), \theta)\Delta t) \right]\]

where \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \boldsymbol{\sigma}\boldsymbol{\sigma}^T \Delta t\) is the covariance matrix.

```julia using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL

7 Parameter calibration example

function calibrate_mean_field_model() # Generate “truth” data from ABM function generate_abm_data(α_true, β_true, σ, ntrajs=20) dt = 0.1 tmax = 10.0 times = 0:dt:tmax x0 = 10.0

    trajectories = zeros(ntrajs, length(times))
    
    for traj in 1:ntrajs
        x = x0
        for (i, t) in enumerate(times)
            trajectories[traj, i] = x
            if i < length(times)
                dx = α_true * x * (1 - x/β_true) * dt + σ * x * sqrt(dt) * randn()
                x = max(0.1, x + dx)  # Prevent extinction
            end
        end
    end
    
    return times, trajectories
end

# True parameters
α_true, β_true, σ = 0.1, 50.0, 0.2
times, abm_trajectories = generate_abm_data(α_true, β_true, σ)

# Mean-field model (logistic growth)
function logistic_ode!(du, u, p, t)
    α, β = p
    du[1] = α * u[1] * (1 - u[1]/β)
end

# Objective function: minimize distance to ABM statistics
function objective(
